
::: {.paper-table}
**Table 30. C.3 Stereotype effects and implicit attitudes**

| Finding | Published effect | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Stereotype threat (standardized-test performance) | d > 0.5 in many published studies | Mentioning demographics before a test substantially alters group-average performance; much of the Black–White test-score gap is a priming effect | Steele & Aronson [@steele1995] and a large derivative literature |
| IAT predicts discriminatory behavior | original r =0.274 | A 5-minute reaction-time task measures unconscious attitudes that cause real-world discrimination | Greenwald, McGhee & Schwartz [@greenwald1998] and derivatives |
| Implicit bias training changes behavior | substantial short-run | Brief training meaningfully reduces discriminatory behavior in hiring, policing, medicine | Widely assumed; largely unreplicated |
| Math-prime stereotype vulnerability (Asian women) | substantial | Priming either “Asian” or “woman” before a math test substantially shifts performance | Shih, Pittinsky & Ambady [@shih1999] |
| Soccer-referee red cards and skin tone | OR ≈ 1.4 in typical specification | Professional referees issue disciplinary cards at meaningfully different rates based on player skin tone | Silberzahn et al. [@silberzahn2018] original data; depended heavily on analytic choices |
:::
